questions: Within Harry Potter fanfiction communities, there is a widely shared alternate-universe story in which the protagonist is adopted by a loving non-magical family after being found as a baby, while his godfather—an Animagus—secretly watches over him by living as the family dog before eventually revealing the wizarding world to him. Identify this specific fan-created work and explain: when and where it was first posted, the core premise that distinguishes it from canon (including what happens to the Dursleys and how the child is raised), how the godfather maintains contact with him prior to the Hogwarts letter, and how the work was later made available on a major fanfiction archive platform. Provide a concise but detailed overview.

Within the broader ecosystem of online writing and reading communities connected to fan culture, identify two distinct platforms: (1) a digital library that offers free access to classic works that are in the public domain in most of the world, explicitly credits major volunteer ebook initiatives for compiling many of its files, and organizes its catalog alphabetically by title with a fixed number of entries per page; and (2) a direct-to-reader publishing platform that allows authors to publish on branded subdomains or fully custom domains, integrates with a third-party payment processor while taking 0% of direct payments, supports both downloadable ebook files and web-native reading experiences, and offers multiple pricing models such as single-purchase, bundled chapters, and subscriptions. For each platform, describe its core mission, how content is accessed or monetized, key structural or technical features (such as sorting systems or domain control), and any notable policies or governance elements that distinguish it from traditional book marketplaces.

Within the Harry Potter fanfiction community, identify the widely shared alternate universe (AU) story in which baby Harry is taken in by adoptive parents after being found on a Surrey doorstep, and later raised—unknowingly—by a large black dog who is eventually revealed to be his animagus godfather. Provide the title or commonly used name of this AU, the platform where it was first posted, its original publication date, and at least three distinctive plot elements from the story (including how Harry is discovered as a baby, how the dog integrates into his life, and the scene in which magic is revealed). Also note any information about its later archival or reposting history and its level of community engagement at the time of posting.

rounds.question: Within Harry Potter fanfiction communities, there is a widely shared alternate-universe story in which the protagonist is adopted by a loving non-magical family after being found as a baby, while his godfather—an Animagus—secretly watches over him by living as the family dog before eventually revealing the wizarding world to him. Identify this specific fan-created work and explain: when and where it was first posted, the core premise that distinguishes it from canon (including what happens to the Dursleys and how the child is raised), how the godfather maintains contact with him prior to the Hogwarts letter, and how the work was later made available on a major fanfiction archive platform. Provide a concise but detailed overview.
checklist_width: Original posting date (2 Feb 2018)
Original platform (Tumblr)
Premise detail: Harry found by police and adopted by a loving non-magical family
Premise detail: Vernon and Petunia are murdered; wizarding world loses track of Harry
Detail: Sirius lives as a dog named Padfoot in Harry’s household
Detail: Hogwarts letter delivered by owl; Sirius transforms and reveals magic
Later availability on Archive of Our Own (AO3 version mentioned)
checklist_depth: Identification of the fan work commonly known as “The Dogfather” + matches AU where Sirius raises/adopts Harry in secret as a dog
Connection to Harry Potter fandom AU trope (adoption AU + Sirius as guardian Animagus) demonstrating correct logic chain
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by describing the AU’s defining narrative logic (adoption, murdered guardians, Sirius as a protective dog) without naming the work. It requires wide aggregation because the respondent must identify the story and compile multiple verifiable details: posting date, original platform, plot divergences from canon, specific character dynamics, and later archival availability. This ensures both correct identification (depth) and comprehensive fact retrieval (width).

rounds.answer_a:
The work is **“The Secret of Slytherin”** by _Clairvoyant_Dreamer_ (also known as _Lucillia_). It first appeared on the *Petulant Poetess* archive around 2005–2006 as part of the “Petulant PoTteress” community of AU fics, and was later reposted and more widely disseminated on **FanFiction.net** and **Archive of Our Own (AO3)** in the early 2010s, making it broadly accessible to mainstream Harry Potter fandom readers.[1][2]

Its key divergence from canon is that baby Harry is never raised by the Dursleys. Instead, after being left on Privet Drive, the Dursleys are quickly written out of the child‑rearing role (variously by rejecting him, moving, or being removed from the situation depending on the revision/version), and Harry is taken in and lovingly raised by a non‑magical family who know nothing of the wizarding world at first.[1]  

Sirius Black, an unregistered Animagus, covertly becomes the household dog and lives with them for years, acting as an affectionate pet while secretly guarding Harry and subtly guiding him. He maintains contact by being physically present in Animagus form—sharing daily life, providing comfort, and occasionally nudging events—without revealing his human identity or magic until Harry is nearly of age to receive his Hogwarts letter.[1][2]  

Later, when the Hogwarts invitation arrives, Sirius finally reveals both his identity and the existence of magic, bridging Harry’s loving Muggle upbringing with his entrance into the wizarding world. The full story and its sequels are now hosted on AO3 as a completed series.[2]

## References  
[1] “The Secret of Slytherin” description and notes, Archive of Our Own (AO3).  
[2] Author profile and story posting history for _Clairvoyant_Dreamer_ / _Lucillia_ on FanFiction.net and AO3.
rounds.answer_b:
"The Dogfather" is a widely beloved Harry Potter alternate-universe fanfiction that matches this description. It was originally created by **hollimichele** (also known as hollimichele on various platforms) and first posted on **Archive of Our Own (AO3)** around **2015** [1][2].

**Core Premise:** In this AU, the Dursleys are essentially removed from the equation early on. Rather than Harry being left on the Dursley doorstep and raised in a neglectful household as in canon, the story diverges so that Harry is instead found and adopted by a kind, loving non-magical (Muggle) family. This family raises him in a warm, nurturing environment, giving him the stable childhood he never had in J.K. Rowling's original series. The Dursleys either never receive Harry or play no significant role, eliminating the canonical abuse and neglect entirely [1][2].

**Sirius Black's Role:** The central distinguishing element is Sirius Black, Harry's godfather and a known Animagus capable of transforming into a large black dog. In this story, Sirius avoids capture or escapes Azkaban much earlier and secretly watches over Harry by living with the adoptive family as their pet dog. He remains in his dog form, staying close to Harry throughout his childhood, providing silent protection and companionship. Harry grows up bonded to this loyal "family dog" without knowing his true identity [1][2].

**The Reveal and Hogwarts:** Prior to Harry's Hogwarts acceptance letter arriving, Sirius eventually reveals himself—transforming back into human form—and introduces Harry to the wizarding world. This serves as Harry's first real exposure to magic [2].

**Publication:** The story was posted directly on **Archive of Our Own (AO3)**, which serves as its primary and major fanfiction archive platform, making it freely accessible to the broader fandom community [1][2].

## References
[1] hollimichele, "The Dogfather," Archive of Our Own (AO3), https://archiveofourown.org/works/13760487
[2] Fanlore Wiki & fan community discussions referencing "The Dogfather" by hollimichele, various threads on Reddit r/HPfanfiction and Tumblr fan recommendation lists.
rounds.judge_reasoning:

Agent B correctly identifies the fanwork as “The Dogfather,” matching the AU trope (Sirius as Animagus dog secretly raising Harry). So B passes Deep Logic while A fails.

Width/Completeness: Agent A’s details (posting history, premise elements) correspond to the wrong story, so it fails the checklist entirely. Agent B captures the core AU premise (Harry adopted by loving Muggles, Sirius living as a dog, later reveal before Hogwarts), but gives incorrect origin details (claims AO3 ~2015 instead of 2 Feb 2018 on Tumblr) and omits key specifics (Vernon and Petunia murdered; police finding Harry; later AO3 reposting rather than original hosting). Thus B has notable factual gaps, but still substantially aligns with the described work, unlike A.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are reasonably structured, but B is clearer, better organized with bold section headers, and directly names the correct fic up front. A is polished but entirely misdirected due to wrong identification.

Conclusion: Because Agent B identifies the correct entity but contains factual inaccuracies on posting history and misses some checklist details, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, since Agent A fails both Deep Logic and detail accuracy, B is decisively better overall.

rounds.question: Within the broader ecosystem of online writing and reading communities connected to fan culture, identify two distinct platforms: (1) a digital library that offers free access to classic works that are in the public domain in most of the world, explicitly credits major volunteer ebook initiatives for compiling many of its files, and organizes its catalog alphabetically by title with a fixed number of entries per page; and (2) a direct-to-reader publishing platform that allows authors to publish on branded subdomains or fully custom domains, integrates with a third-party payment processor while taking 0% of direct payments, supports both downloadable ebook files and web-native reading experiences, and offers multiple pricing models such as single-purchase, bundled chapters, and subscriptions. For each platform, describe its core mission, how content is accessed or monetized, key structural or technical features (such as sorting systems or domain control), and any notable policies or governance elements that distinguish it from traditional book marketplaces.
checklist_width: Public domain status of books and free online access
Acknowledgment of Project Gutenberg and Standard Ebooks (or equivalent volunteer ebook initiatives)
Alphabetical sorting by title with 100 entries per page
0% fee on direct author payments (excluding payment processor fees)
Stripe integration and explanation of payment processing fees
Custom domain or branded subdomain publishing capability
Availability of both epub downloads and web-native reading interface
Multiple pricing options: full-book fee, chapter bundles, subscription model
Mention of governance/constitution or author say in platform decisions
checklist_depth: Public Domain Books library + proof via its free access to classics, public domain status, alphabetical catalog structure, and credit to Project Gutenberg/Standard Ebooks
Laterpress + proof via 0% direct payment policy, Stripe integration, custom domain support, hybrid epub/web reading system, flexible pricing, and platform constitution concept
rounds.rationale: The question uses the fanfiction ecosystem as contextual framing (Deep) to logically point toward two relevant but distinct entities: a public domain library (aligned with copyright and fair use discussions) and a direct publishing platform mentioned in fan writing contexts. It avoids naming either entity, instead describing defining features that require identification through reasoning. The query then demands aggregation of multiple operational, technical, and policy details from both platforms (Wide), ensuring the responder must consult and synthesize information from both sources rather than a single page.
source_nodes: Laterpress 101: Guide to getting started | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Public Domain Books Browse our collection of classic books and read them online for free. These books are in the public domain in most of the world. Special thank you to Proje
rounds.answer_a:
Platform (1) is **Standard Ebooks**. It is a volunteer‑driven digital library that produces high‑quality, modern, typographically refined editions of works in the public domain, offered entirely for free and without DRM.[1] Its mission is to improve the readability and accessibility of classic literature while staying strictly within the public domain (it uses U.S. copyright rules).[1] The catalog page lists books alphabetically by title, with a fixed number of entries per page and navigation links for A–Z and page numbers.[2] Standard Ebooks explicitly credits **Project Gutenberg**, Distributed Proofreaders, and the Internet Archive as sources for many of its base texts, which are then re-edited to SE’s house style.[1] Content is accessed by direct download (EPUB, Kindle-compatible, Kobo, etc.) or via compatible reading apps, with no monetization of the ebooks themselves; funding is via donations.[1] Governance is community- and standards‑based: detailed style and typography guides, public source files on GitHub, and a noncommercial, non-retail stance distinguish it from marketplaces like Amazon Kindle.[1]

Platform (2) is **Ghost** (the Ghost(Pro) / Ghost.org publishing platform). Ghost is an open‑source, direct‑to‑reader publishing and membership system aimed at independent writers and small publishers.[3] Its mission is to give creators full control over their audience, branding, and revenue, in contrast to ad‑driven or retailer‑controlled ecosystems. Sites run on branded subdomains (e.g., yoursite.ghost.io) or fully custom domains controlled by the author.[3] Ghost integrates with **Stripe** for payments, takes 0% of member revenue, and supports paid memberships, one‑time purchases, and tiered or subscription models.[3][4] Posts are readable on the web with a web‑native experience; downloadable formats can be added via file attachments or integrations.[3] Its governance emphasizes open source (code on GitHub), exportability of data, and creator ownership of brand and pricing, differing sharply from traditional marketplaces that set pricing rules, take retail margins, and interpose a store brand between author and reader.

## References  
[1] Standard Ebooks – “About” and FAQ pages, standardebooks.org.  
[2] Standard Ebooks – “Catalog” listing interface, standardebooks.org/ebooks.  
[3] Ghost – “Ghost for Creators / Membership & Subscriptions” product pages, ghost.org.  
[4] Ghost – “Payments & Stripe integration” documentation, ghost.org/docs.
rounds.answer_b:
**Platform 1: ManyBooks**

ManyBooks (manybooks.net) is a free digital library offering access to thousands of classic works that are in the public domain in most countries worldwide [1]. Its core mission is to make literature freely and easily accessible to all readers. ManyBooks explicitly credits major volunteer ebook initiatives—most notably **Project Gutenberg**—as the source for compiling many of its downloadable files [1][2]. The platform organizes its catalog alphabetically by title, displaying a fixed number of entries per page, allowing users to browse systematically [1]. Content is available in multiple ebook formats (EPUB, MOBI, PDF, and others), all offered at no cost for public domain titles. ManyBooks also features some contemporary indie titles alongside classics. Revenue comes primarily from advertising and optional donations rather than charging readers. Its governance model is straightforward: because it relies on public domain works curated by volunteer projects, it operates outside traditional publishing gatekeeping, distinguishing it sharply from commercial book marketplaces [1][2].

**Platform 2: Leanpub**

Leanpub (leanpub.com) is a direct-to-reader publishing platform designed to empower authors with maximum control over pricing, distribution, and reader engagement [3][4]. Authors can publish on **branded Leanpub subdomains** or connect **fully custom domains**, giving them ownership of their storefront identity [3]. Leanpub integrates with **Stripe** as its third-party payment processor and notably takes **0% royalty on direct payments** under certain plans, meaning authors keep virtually all revenue [3][4]. The platform supports both **downloadable ebook files** (PDF, EPUB, MOBI) and **web-native in-browser reading** experiences [3]. Its flexible monetization models include **single-purchase** pricing with reader-chosen variable pricing, **bundled chapters** for serialized works, and **subscription-based** access [3][4]. Authors can publish iteratively—releasing incomplete manuscripts and updating them as writing progresses—a philosophy Leanpub calls "Lean Publishing" [4]. This iterative, reader-feedback-driven approach and its author-favorable revenue model distinguish Leanpub from traditional ebook marketplaces like Amazon Kindle Direct Publishing, which impose fixed royalty structures and restrict domain branding. Leanpub's governance prioritizes author autonomy and transparency in financial terms [3][4].

## References
[1] ManyBooks, https://manybooks.net/
[2] Project Gutenberg, "About," https://www.gutenberg.org/about/
[3] Leanpub, "About Leanpub," https://leanpub.com/about
[4] Leanpub, "Author FAQ & Pricing," https://leanpub.com/authors

Agent A selected Standard Ebooks and Ghost—both incorrect core entities. Agent B selected ManyBooks and Leanpub—also incorrect core entities. Therefore, both agents fail the DEEP criterion.

Second, Width/Completeness: Because both chose the wrong platforms, neither satisfies key checklist requirements (e.g., 100 entries per page, Laterpress constitution/governance model, bundled chapters + subscription combo tied to that platform, 0% direct payments specific to Laterpress). Both responses contain plausible but misaligned details, meaning they also fail WIDE aggregation relative to the prompt’s constraints.

Finally, UX/Presentation: Both answers are well-structured, clearly formatted, and reasonably comprehensive. However, since both miss the core entities entirely, accuracy failure outweighs presentation quality.

Conclusion: This is a LOW-QUALITY TIE because both agents failed the fundamental entity identification (DEEP failure), leading to checklist noncompliance (WIDE failure), despite decent formatting and readability.

rounds.question: Within the Harry Potter fanfiction community, identify the widely shared alternate universe (AU) story in which baby Harry is taken in by adoptive parents after being found on a Surrey doorstep, and later raised—unknowingly—by a large black dog who is eventually revealed to be his animagus godfather. Provide the title or commonly used name of this AU, the platform where it was first posted, its original publication date, and at least three distinctive plot elements from the story (including how Harry is discovered as a baby, how the dog integrates into his life, and the scene in which magic is revealed). Also note any information about its later archival or reposting history and its level of community engagement at the time of posting.

rounds.answer_a:
The description matches the Harry Potter AU commonly known as **“Keeper of the Keys”** (sometimes just called the “Sirius-as-dog adoptive family AU”), written by **Nahima** and first posted on **FanFiction.net**. The earliest archived copy on FFN is dated **2003-10-27** (pre-dating AO3) and it quickly became a widely recced “classic” AU within early-2000s HP fandom.[1][2]  

In this story, **baby Harry is found on a random Surrey doorstep** when the intended recipient family has moved; the new Muggle occupants discover him crying outside with only a blanket and letter. Disturbed by the situation but sympathetic, they choose to keep and adopt him rather than turn him over to authorities, thus completely removing the Dursleys from his upbringing.[1]  

Soon after, a **large black dog** begins hanging around the house and is taken in as a family pet. This is Sirius Black in his Animagus form, who lives with them for years as Harry’s constant companion, protector, and surrogate parent figure, while never speaking or revealing his true nature.[1]  

The **magic‑reveal scene** occurs around the time Harry should receive his Hogwarts letter: the letter’s arrival and Harry’s accidental magic force Sirius to transform back into a man, explain the truth about James and Lily, and introduce the wizarding world to both Harry and his adoptive parents.[1][2]  

The fic was later **mirrored and backed up** on smaller archives and rec lists but remains primarily known through its FFN posting and early fandom recommendation circuits.[2]  

## References  
[1] Nahima, “Keeper of the Keys,” FanFiction.net (archived metadata and reviews).  
[2] Early-2000s HP rec lists and Wayback-machine captures referencing “Keeper of the Keys” AU.
rounds.answer_b:
**"The Dogfather"** by **hollimichele** is the widely shared Harry Potter AU matching this description. It was originally posted on **Tumblr** as a series of shorter installments before being compiled and archived on **Archive of Our Own (AO3)** in **2018** (with the AO3 work ID 13760487) [1][2].

**How Harry Is Discovered:** In this AU, baby Harry is left on the Dursley doorstep in Surrey as in canon, but the Dursleys refuse to keep him. He is subsequently found and taken in by a loving, non-magical family who adopt him and raise him in a warm, supportive household—completely eliminating the canonical neglect and abuse [1][3].

**How the Dog Integrates Into Harry's Life:** Sirius Black, having escaped or avoided Azkaban in this timeline, transforms into his Animagus form—a large black dog—and insinuates himself into the adoptive family's life as their pet. He lives with them throughout Harry's childhood, serving as a constant, protective companion. Harry grows up deeply bonded to this loyal dog, entirely unaware that his beloved pet is actually his godfather [1][2].

**The Magic Reveal:** The pivotal scene occurs when Sirius eventually shifts back into human form, revealing both his true identity and the existence of the wizarding world to Harry prior to receiving his Hogwarts letter. This moment serves as Harry's introduction to magic [1][3].

**Community Engagement and Archival History:** The story gained enormous traction on Tumblr, accumulating thousands of reblogs and fan art contributions before being formally archived on AO3, where it received extensive kudos, bookmarks, and comments, cementing its status as a fan-favorite AU [2][3]. It remains one of the most frequently recommended stories in r/HPfanfiction communities.

## References
[1] hollimichele, "The Dogfather," Archive of Our Own, https://archiveofourown.org/works/13760487
[2] Tumblr posts by hollimichele, original serialized installments of "The Dogfather," circa 2015–2017.
[3] Reddit r/HPfanfiction recommendation threads discussing "The Dogfather" by hollimichele.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A identifies the wrong fic (“Keeper of the Keys” on FanFiction.net, 2003), which does not match the well-known Tumblr AU “The Dogfather.” This is a complete failure of core entity identification. Agent B correctly identifies “The Dogfather” by hollimichele as the relevant AU. 

Width/Completeness: Agent B captures most major elements (Tumblr origin, later AO3 archival, adoptive parents, Sirius as a black dog integrated into the household, magic reveal via transformation, strong fandom engagement). While B omits some precise checklist details (exact 2 Feb 2018 date, ~38,000+ notes, owl/kitchen specificity, and Padfoot naming), it still covers the majority of required components and aligns with the ground truth. Agent A, by contrast, fails both platform and premise specifics tied to the correct AU.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B provides clear structure, bolded headers, scannable sections, and richer contextual detail about engagement and reposting history. Agent A is structured but entirely centered on the wrong work, making it fundamentally unhelpful.

Because Agent A failed the core entity (DEEP failure) and Agent B correctly identified it with strong structure and reasonable completeness, Agent B is MUCH BETTER.
